writeTurn.prompt: write me an article for pool owners based on this real Aquatrace job scenario: a homeowner had unexplained daily water loss, Aquatrace checked the pool, pressure testing pointed to a leaking return line, and the article should explain why pressure testing matters without naming the customer
writeTurn.answer:
# Why Is My Pool Losing Water? What Pressure Testing Reveals

If you've noticed your pool water level dropping faster than evaporation could explain, you're not alone — and it's worth taking seriously before it turns into a bigger repair.

## The Scenario

A pool owner reached out about steady, unexplained water loss. Nothing was visibly wrong — no cracks, no wet spots, no obvious signs of trouble. The pool just kept losing water, day after day.

Our team came out to investigate. Rather than guessing or starting with a slab-cracking search, we ran a pressure test on the plumbing lines. That test pointed us straight to a leaking return line — the pipe that carries filtered water back into the pool.

## Why Pressure Testing Comes First

A lot of water loss gets blamed on evaporation, splash-out, or a cracked pool shell. But plumbing leaks are one of the most common — and most overlooked — causes. Here's why testing the lines matters:

- **It isolates the problem fast.** Pressure testing tells us whether the leak is in the plumbing (skimmer, main drain, return lines) or somewhere else, like the pool structure or equipment pad.
- **It prevents unnecessary digging or repairs.** Without a pressure test, it's tempting to guess — and guessing can mean cutting into decking or replacing equipment that was never the problem.
- **It catches leaks you can't see.** Return line leaks are often underground or behind the pool wall. There's no visible sign until the water bill or level drop gives it away.
- **It saves water and money.** A slow, steady leak adds up. Left unaddressed, it can also erode soil around the pool structure, leading to bigger structural issues over time.

## How It Works

We pressurize each line individually and monitor for pressure drop. A line that won't hold pressure has a break, crack, or failed fitting somewhere along its run. Once we know which line is compromised, we can pinpoint the repair — instead of tearing up your whole yard to find it.

## When to Call

If you're topping off your pool more than about an inch a week (beyond normal evaporation for your climate), it's worth having the lines checked. Catching a return line leak early usually means a straightforward repair — waiting on it can mean bigger excavation and higher costs down the road.

*Noticing unexplained water loss in your own pool? A pressure test is the fastest way to get a real answer instead of a guess.*
